human_verification.notes: Reviewed the package for threat-intelligence-specific planning, source hygiene, evidence traceability, ATT&CK mapping, capacity and allocation recommendations, refusal boundaries, functionality coverage, research contract completeness, and validated spec-pack posture.

specialist_owned_evidence: Boundary is explicit to CTI planning, source evaluation, indicator interpretation, threat assessment, ATT&CK mapping, confidence handling, allocation recommendations, and handoff packaging rather than generic cybersecurity operations.

Authority sources cover NIST SP 800-150, NIST CSF 2.0, CISA advisories, CISA AIS, CISA KEV, MITRE ATT&CK, FIRST TLP, FIRST CSIRT Services Framework, OASIS STIX/TAXII, NIST SP 800-30, NIST SP 800-53, CIS Controls, OWASP logging guidance, SEC disclosure rules, and FTC Safeguards Rule caveats.

Evaluation scenarios cover normal CTI analysis, indicator confidence, ATT&CK mapping, capacity constraints, demand exceeding capacity, conflicting priorities, final approval refusal, partial evidence, source contradiction, penetration testing refusal, incident response execution refusal, audit attestation refusal, architecture boundary, malware analysis handoff, tenant adaptation, TLP and regulated-disclosure boundaries, CTI requirements planning, mitigation guardrails, and regression behavior.
The package is organized under catalog/cybersecurity/threat-intelligence-specialist/ and keeps runtime strategy specification-based without claiming retained implementation ownership.

review_notes: No unreviewed regressions were identified in the local package review.
Functionality coverage is complete in both evaluation/results.json and readiness/evidence.json as required by the issue.
The scenario suite exceeds the legacy minimum of 12 scenarios and preserves strict validation posture.

notes: This package is suitable as a validated threat intelligence specialist for CTI planning, prioritization, evidence-backed analysis, source hygiene, recommendation guardrails, and handoff routing.
Runtime remains specification-based until a retained implementation is promoted separately under explicit frequent-use criteria.
